chapters.title: Learn the truth about love as it is
chapters.text:
Love doesn't have to make you cry or lose sleep. It shouldn't feel like a battle. As soon as you avoid the temptation to hide from your true self, you will find a way to be honest about any relationship. The transgender activist Juno Dawson points out that wrestling with herself left no headspace for other things, including love.

A couples therapist, Esther Perel, says the common cause of infidelity is people wondering, “Could I be any happier?” They somehow apply the feeling of newness and excitement to finding someone who will be responsible for all their expectations. Conversely, a betrayed person who is not curious about an affair shows a challenging dynamic. However, each party must display empathy to understand the other's experience when working through adultery.

Feeling connected is essential for people’s lives. Sarah Hepola, author of the bestselling memoir Blackout, thinks we have to balance the art of being alone with the idea of companionship. Our world is slowly shifting from marriage and parenthood to one where various lifestyles are allowed.

Focusing on what we already have helps ward off longing.

What is often dismissed, for example, is a family love we take for granted — parents who live nearby and are easily accessible. We forget that the most remarkable love story of our lives will be over one day, and how can we carry the loss when the world demands happiness 24/7? Also, how do we switch on the diversity of emotion and memory in children?

For Diana Evans, a novelist who explores the influence of domesticity on women, motherhood is a shift from a physical to an emotional connection. Looking at her sixteen-year-old daughter, she recalls her as a newborn, a toddler, and a six-year-old. The relationship Evans and her child made together enriches the feeling of gratitude for her being a mother. The bond journalist Poorna Bell made with her sister is crucial in the same way; there is no need to ask a thousand questions to understand their feelings for each other because of everyday communication.

Compassion is essential to this kind of love; if it feels comfortable, there is no need to look for “bigger” happiness.

chapters.title: Identify your needs to create meaningful connections
chapters.text:
The Conversations on Love creator met her very first love at school. It was not a genuine feeling but a teenage affection. She created the thrilling chapters in her mind, but there was nothing to hold on to in real life. She spent time trying to recollect the minor details: the position of a freckle on his arm or the way he spread butter on toast. It was something people call “chemistry.”

Pursuing love in her twenties, Natasha Lunn describes two types of people:

• Those who easily fall into a relationship but also feel happy when single.
• Others who can't get past the starting block of a relationship and struggle to find happiness independently.

Everyone has experienced an intense passion - when dream trumps reality, and expectancy feels better than knowing. In early adolescence, people take the scant details of an ordinary connection and create an imaginary world inside it. But, according to the writer, Ayisha Malik, expecting someone else to fill a hole inside you is too much. It’s not their job; it's your job to recreate the idealized version you look for in others.

If I do meet someone, he will be a normal human being … who is flawed, and who will make mistakes just as I will. ~ Ayisha Malik

Alain de Botton, philosopher, and the School of Life founder, says people often think they need a relationship. But in reality, they need a connection; meaningless searches for love bring distraction. The efforts poured into longing should be used to dig into a love already there. It's loving a partner and purposeful solitude: creating things that connect people. Vivid examples are family and friendship — love that deserves learning and understanding.

Sometimes, it is better to be in solitude than emotionally alone in a relationship.

The psychology of being alone can be humiliating. We don't feel as lonely on Monday night as on Saturday night. However, in unstable relationships, we are at the mercy of chance, letting others abuse our desperation. We overemphasize romantic love because of fairy tales. De Botton pinpoints that the tragedy of being alone is not about loneliness but the stories we tell ourselves.

chapters.title: Love isn't just about romance
chapters.text:
The love we have yet to explore is friendship. The ancient Greeks used the term “philia” to describe a special bond allowing people to embrace self-knowledge and deepen their self-esteem. Malik recalls how the company in her twenties expanded her view of love. Psychotherapist Philippa Perry feels grateful to friends at work who gave her the courage to leave a toxic relationship.

Podcast host Dolly Alderton suggests that being a friend in your twenties is not the same as in your thirties or forties. It’s easier to belong to a friend when you're twenty-eight and live three bus stops away from each other. You look for an identity in your twenties but must declare one in your thirties. It is because you have more responsibilities, like children, a sick parent, or a business in a different country. Therefore, old friendships need adjustments to your current life pace if you want to make them work.

Unfortunately, there's a misalignment in the hierarchy between love for a partner and devotion to a friend. Candice Carty-Williams, the author of the bestselling novel Queenie, says friends were the first people she could lean on. Despite the different dynamics and personalities, they shaped a family she always wanted. She thinks being a good friend requires as much time and love as any romantic interaction. But you must be aware of a person's history and desires to accept the different facets of who they are.

Being emotionally deprived, people often replace love with less pleasant feelings like anger, guilt, and frustration.

Poet, author, and broadcaster Lemn Sissay shares the opposite experience of Carty-Williams. Raised without a family, Sissay’s foster parents rejected him at 12 years old without explanation. Growing older, he embraced love in the family as an integral part of his personality but struggled to comprehend the past and avoid being steered by it. Sissay reflects on his emotional deprivation as a trigger to understanding how important love is and that it’s in every action: physical touch, picking up the phone, and saying what you need to say right now. Therefore, if you struggle to find a romantic partner, there is no need to worry; spot the connections you’ve made so far!

chapters.title: Accept your partner's transformation to build strong relationships
chapters.text:
For some reason, we assume the most romantic period in a couple's life, the honeymoon, is followed by a darker, thornier patch. Suddenly, the fairy tale part of the relationship is over, and it's just gray weekdays and the trials of life. The newlywed life seems like hard work to satisfy a partner's needs while neglecting our own. So, we frame the whole marriage into a picture of tiredness and disappointment, unable to accept our partner's transformation over time because of our lack of self-reflection.

Lunn and her husband Dan had a terrible miscarriage that kept them from going on their honeymoon. This situation greatly affected their married life, which differed from the first dates with kisses under the moon. However, their experience taught them that partnerships are not just for smiles and joy but also for pain and grief.

You don’t ever really choose a person because they change, and your lives do too. You’re choosing how a person weathers change. ~ Mira Jacob

The secret ingredient for a stable relationship is acceptance. You and your partner will grow and change. You will have new views and ideas that you will not always readily tolerate in each other. It requires effort and daily work. Mira Jacob, the author of the graphic memoir Good Talk, confirms that an old love is more fragile than a new one. When starting a relationship, you know what can go wrong and how to avoid it; the stakes are much higher when maintaining a relationship for years.

Marriage is about getting to know your partner and being ready for their transformation.

Essayist and opinion writer Roxane Gay says being loved means feeling cared for and seen. It's when a partner accepts your feelings, even when they differ. On the contrary, by trying to make yourself better for somebody else, you will never reach a point of stability. After the romantic phase shifts, your partner will be confused to see that your true nature is not as perfect as presented initially.

Did you know? The expression “honeymoon” comes from two Old English words: “hony” as a period of tenderness for a newlywed couple, and “moone” as the inevitable shift of that pleasure, like the sudden change of a moon phase.

chapters.title: The major enemy of a long-term relationship
chapters.text:
We imagine love to be tense and heartbreaking, but it's not. Roxane Gay says it’s an effort that doesn’t feel like work; it is not hard to commit at the right time with the right person. A journalist, Christina Patterson, suggests love is far from climbing Everest. It’s just a series of daily decisions, so we don't take our loved ones for granted.

To connect with someone emotionally, we need to maintain healthy physical contact. The opinions here differ. Experts from psychology, medicine, and other areas of life view sex as the measure of love. A sex educator, Emily Nagoski, defines two types of sex drive:

• Out-of-the-blue interest in sex
• Responsive desire

We act selflessly in a long-term relationship because we feel secure.

The attachment mechanism in the brain is likely to force a chemical response at the beginning of a relationship. We want sex more often, but the truth is that we feel less safe using sex as a reason to be closer. It also explains a popular stereotype: people don't want sex as frequently after years in a relationship. When a relationship is safe, the brain doesn't need to strengthen the closeness. However, if we see a threat (like being away from a partner for a few weeks), sex becomes a tool to shorten the distance.

According to relationship coach Susan Quilliam, couples who abuse each other are “enmeshed.” It means they're so tight that any contention feels like a betrayal. While we find similarities in a partner during the initial stage of a relationship, we try to individuate as this relationship progresses. At this point, you can keep nagging each other or shut down without a connection.

Peggy Kleinplatz, a psychologist from Canada, describes the characteristics of great sex:

• Being genuine, authentic, and transparent
• Exposing your vulnerability
• Sharing your true desires habitually from the beginning

Sharing your most sensitive sides with your partner is difficult sometimes, so don't push yourself too hard. Open up at a comfortable pace, and take care of your feelings. As a result, you’ll secure a bond with your partner and will not perceive sex as the only measure of a successful relationship.

chapters.title: Share your vulnerability to stay close
chapters.text:
Love doesn't have to make you cry or lose sleep. It shouldn't feel like a battle. As soon as you avoid the temptation to hide from your true self, you will find a way to be honest about any relationship. The transgender activist Juno Dawson points out that wrestling with herself left no headspace for other things, including love.

A couples therapist, Esther Perel, says the common cause of infidelity is people wondering, “Could I be any happier?” They somehow apply the feeling of newness and excitement to finding someone who will be responsible for all their expectations. Conversely, a betrayed person who is not curious about an affair shows a challenging dynamic. However, each party must display empathy to understand the other's experience when working through adultery.

Feeling connected is essential for people’s lives. Sarah Hepola, author of the bestselling memoir Blackout, thinks we have to balance the art of being alone with the idea of companionship. Our world is slowly shifting from marriage and parenthood to one where various lifestyles are allowed.

Focusing on what we already have helps ward off longing.

What is often dismissed, for example, is a family love we take for granted — parents who live nearby and are easily accessible. We forget that the most remarkable love story of our lives will be over one day, and how can we carry the loss when the world demands happiness 24/7? Also, how do we switch on the diversity of emotion and memory in children?

For Diana Evans, a novelist who explores the influence of domesticity on women, motherhood is a shift from a physical to an emotional connection. Looking at her sixteen-year-old daughter, she recalls her as a newborn, a toddler, and a six-year-old. The relationship Evans and her child made together enriches the feeling of gratitude for her being a mother. The bond journalist Poorna Bell made with her sister is crucial in the same way; there is no need to ask a thousand questions to understand their feelings for each other because of everyday communication.

Compassion is essential to this kind of love; if it feels comfortable, there is no need to look for “bigger” happiness.

chapters.title: Loss is painful, but acknowledging it helps to heal
chapters.text:
Suffering comes first after loss; there is no need to explain why it’s a necessary condition to complete a love puzzle. Psychotherapist Stephen Grosz expands the definition of suffering by separating the pain we experience after the loss and the post-pain state, keeping us in self-pity. He draws life as a series of losses we can survive: changing schools, universities, and jobs. The positive aspects of all those situations are about embracing new adventures and starting a new phase in life.

On the contrary, losing love is an unfair trade as we replace happiness and lightness with grief. Journalist Melanie Reid was seriously injured during a horse-riding accident and is now paralyzed from the chest down. She accessed more profound love through the quiet kindness of strangers and was surprised how the love she took as something normal became the source of inspiration for her and for people she had never seen before.

Talking about death is love; we should all do it more.

Actor Greg Wise noted this principle when his sister died from cancer in 2016. He remembered his sister's last days on Earth as the most complicated. She didn't want anybody but him beside her deathbed; the agony made her unable to accept visitors or have difficult conversations. She still needed him to stay focused and responsible, which would be tough without a powerful support system. On the contrary, writer Joe Hammond (who died of motor neuron disease) confessed that physical inactivity made him set aside his ego and become more honest with his friends.

Lisa Taddeo, the author of the extraordinary novel Three Women, believes we experience grief first and are devastated by the echo following. Losing her father made Lisa look for men with good parents to ease the pain. She learned that being judgmental to others is simply bizarre, while acknowledging pain can be a part of how people heal.

Somehow, we are embarrassed to look like helpless creatures, but sharing a harmful experience is a way to respond appropriately.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
What have we learned about love? It's a complicated and fragile matter, constantly present in our lives, even when it seems invisible. Some people get lucky with love at first sight, while others need another glance. Love is undoubtedly beyond the teenage drama from high school years, but it shouldn't repress the ability to show that someone is as vulnerable as we are. The worst enemies standing between our relationship goals and hopes are self-pity and ego, so we make the wrong choices and end up with the wrong people. Loving and being loved should be a daily routine, like a workout session.

There is also an underestimated form of love, such as family and friendship. Happy parents, siblings, and old and new friends are as important as romantic interactions. Love doesn't mean belonging to one person; it's about a shared responsibility between people who play a significant role in your life.

The thing everyone wants is to love and be loved. Despite society’s influence on dreams, like career success or raising an income, people struggle for attention deep down. We're afraid of exposing our worst sides to our partners, but the truth is that we should let go of this fear and be ready to take responsibility. What can you do now to change the perception of love and discover hidden treasures?

Try this


1. Follow small rituals to renew the romance of a relationship, such as planning a birthday itinerary or breakfast in a bakery on weekends.
2. Think about what you want in a partnership and what role a partner plays in your union.
3. Stop feeling guilty and mistreated; remember, you choose to feel miserable. The best is yet to come, and you must believe in it!

Read it till the end?
